tossups.question: In a work by this artist, the title figure’s soul is shown being carried to heaven by miniature angels to a similarly small God the Father hovering over the bed in which her body remains. A red girdle trails over a windowsill near a convex mirror in a painting by this man that also features a shelf with a piece of coral and was once believed to depict St. Eligius. Gilt letters “A” hang from a thorny tree in a Madonna and child by this artist. A man with a forked beard against a red background stares straight at the viewer in one painting by this artist of Madonna of the Dry Tree and Portrait of a Young Girl. Hans Memling and this painter of A Goldsmith in His Shop were the leading painters in Bruges (“broozh”) after the death of Jan van Eyck. For 10 points, name this Flemish artist who included a trompe l’oeil (“tromp LOY”) fly in his Portrait of a Carthusian.
tossups.question_sanitized: In a work by this artist, the title figure's soul is shown being carried to heaven by miniature angels to a similarly small God the Father hovering over the bed in which her body remains. A red girdle trails over a windowsill near a convex mirror in a painting by this man that also features a shelf with a piece of coral and was once believed to depict St. Eligius. Gilt letters "A" hang from a thorny tree in a Madonna and child by this artist. A man with a forked beard against a red background stares straight at the viewer in one painting by this artist of Madonna of the Dry Tree and Portrait of a Young Girl. Hans Memling and this painter of A Goldsmith in His Shop were the leading painters in Bruges ("broozh") after the death of Jan van Eyck. For 10 points, name this Flemish artist who included a trompe l'oeil ("tromp LOY") fly in his Portrait of a Carthusian.
tossups.answer: Petrus Christus [or Petrus Christus] (The first work is his Death of the Virgin.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Petrus Christus [or Petrus Christus] (The first work is his Death of the Virgin.)

tossups.question: This artist borrowed Jan van Eyck’s foreshortened-skeleton-flying-over-Hell image from the Last Judgment to produce a version of that scene whose chiaroscuro Hell component is much scarier than van Eyck’s. This man painted portraits of English sitters, like Edward Grimston and a descendant of the Talbot family, though he was based in Bruges, where he was the leading painter after van Eyck and before (**)Hans Memling. 15 gold letter “A” s, representing the Ave Maria prayer, hang from the thorny branches of a dead tree in a small Madonna and child painting by this artist. A fly sits on the trompe-l’oeil frame of a portrait by this artist, whose side-eyed Portrait of a Young Girl shows influence from Rogier van der Weyden, and who influenced (*) Quentin Massys with a genre scene featuring a convex mirror in the bottom right. For 10 points, name this painter of Portrait of a Carthusian and A Goldsmith in his Shop.
tossups.question_sanitized: This artist borrowed Jan van Eyck's foreshortened-skeleton-flying-over-Hell image from the Last Judgment to produce a version of that scene whose chiaroscuro Hell component is much scarier than van Eyck's. This man painted portraits of English sitters, like Edward Grimston and a descendant of the Talbot family, though he was based in Bruges, where he was the leading painter after van Eyck and before (**)Hans Memling. 15 gold letter "A" s, representing the Ave Maria prayer, hang from the thorny branches of a dead tree in a small Madonna and child painting by this artist. A fly sits on the trompe-l'oeil frame of a portrait by this artist, whose side-eyed Portrait of a Young Girl shows influence from Rogier van der Weyden, and who influenced (*) Quentin Massys with a genre scene featuring a convex mirror in the bottom right. For 10 points, name this painter of Portrait of a Carthusian and A Goldsmith in his Shop.

tossups.question: Multiple versions of this person’s Pieta have tools litter the ground as John the Baptist catches a fainting Mary. This painter’s Annunciation takes a birds-eye view in an outdoor setting as it marks the transition from Judaism to Christianity with a change from Gothic to Romanesque architecture. A piece of coral lies against the shelf next to crystal grains in a painting by this artist that, like a Quentin Massys painting, features two men in a convex mirror. In that painting, a man who might be Saint Eligius weighs a wedding ring for a young, wealthy couple. Rogier van der Weyden’s Portrait of a Woman influenced his Portrait of Young Girl, but his most famous portrait shows a fly just below a fork-bearded (*) monk. For 10 points, name this pupil of Jan van Eyck who painted A Goldsmith in his Shop and a portrait of a Carthusian.
tossups.question_sanitized: Multiple versions of this person's Pieta have tools litter the ground as John the Baptist catches a fainting Mary. A piece of coral lies against the shelf next to crystal grains in a painting by this artist that, like a Quentin Massys painting, features two men in a convex mirror. In that painting, a man who might be Saint Eligius weighs a wedding ring for a young, wealthy couple. Rogier van der Weyden's Portrait of a Woman influenced his Portrait of Young Girl, but his most famous portrait shows a fly just below a fork-bearded (*) monk. For 10 points, name this pupil of Jan van Eyck who painted A Goldsmith in his Shop and a portrait of a Carthusian.

bonuses.leadin: One version of this scene was stolen and relocated to Danzig, causing a lawsuit to be placed against the Hanseatic (“han-zee-AT-ick”) League. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: One version of this scene was stolen and relocated to Danzig, causing a lawsuit to be placed against the Hanseatic ("han-zee-AT-ick") League. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this scene. In the aforementioned version, the donor sits on the right-hand scale held by Saint Michael in the foreground. Another version of this scene represents the critic Biagio de Cesena (“B’YA-joh de cheh-ZAY-nah”) as a donkey-eared King Minos and the artist as St. Bartholomew.

Hans Memling’s time as the leading painter of Bruges (“broozh”) was shared with this other artist, who pioneered the use of perspective in Early Netherlandish painting and produced Portrait of a Carthusian (“car-THOO-zee-uhn”).
Memling and Christus rose to prominence after the death of this possible inventor of oil painting, whose own works include the Ghent Altarpiece and the Arnolfini (“ar-noll-FEE-nee”) Wedding.
parts_sanitized: Name this scene. In the aforementioned version, the donor sits on the right-hand scale held by Saint Michael in the foreground. Another version of this scene represents the critic Biagio de Cesena ("B'YA-joh de cheh-ZAY-nah") as a donkey-eared King Minos and the artist as St. Bartholomew.

Hans Memling's time as the leading painter of Bruges ("broozh") was shared with this other artist, who pioneered the use of perspective in Early Netherlandish painting and produced Portrait of a Carthusian ("car-THOO-zee-uhn").
Memling and Christus rose to prominence after the death of this possible inventor of oil painting, whose own works include the Ghent Altarpiece and the Arnolfini ("ar-noll-FEE-nee") Wedding.
answers: The Last Judgement (The versions are by Memling and Michelangelo.)
answers_sanitized: The Last Judgement (The versions are by Memling and Michelangelo.)

bonuses.leadin: One of these animals appears to sit on the frame of a Portrait of a Carthusian in an example of an early Renaissance trompe l’oeil (“tromp LOY”) trick of depicting them. For 10 points each:
parts: Name these animals that represented sin in paintings. According to Vasari, Giotto (“JO-toh”) would fool his teacher Cimabue (“chee-mah-BOO-ay”) by painting these animals hyper-realistically onto portraits by him.

The realistic fly in the Portrait of a Carthusian was painted by this artist, who may have inherited Van Eyck’s workshop and introduced geometric perspective to Flemish painting. He also painted A Goldsmith in His Shop.

A frequent interpretation of the fly is as a moralizing reminder of death, like the vanitas skull paintings in this genre. Dutch artists painted flies and other insects in works in this genre that depict fruits and flowers.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: One of these animals appears to sit on the frame of a Portrait of a Carthusian in an example of an early Renaissance trompe l'oeil ("tromp LOY") trick of depicting them. For 10 points each:

A frequent interpretation of the fly is as a moralizing reminder of death, like the vanitas skull paintings in this genre. Dutch artists painted flies and other insects in works in this genre that depict fruits and flowers.

bonuses.leadin: This man’s painting of the Head of Christ in the Met is based on a now-lost Van Eyck painting, but adds gory new details such as blood dripping down from the Crown of Thorns to Christ’s chest. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this Netherlandish artist who brought geometric perspective and the vanishing point to Northern Europe with his Virgin Enthroned with Saints Jerome and Francis. This artist of Portrait of a Carthusian was Bruges’ leading painter, along with Memling, in the generation after van Eyck.

The medieval craze for the Veil of Veronica, or Holy Face, led many other artists to paint small devotional portraits of Christ, such as this Flemish artist whose Judgment of Cambyses was one of the first Northern Renaissance paintings to include Italian-style putti.
This later artist’s Munich Self-Portrait is a riff on the Holy Face motif. This German artist of Praying Hands and The Four Apostles holds a thistle in an early self-portrait.

This artist of Portrait of a Carthusian was Bruges' leading painter, along with Memling, in the generation after van Eyck.

This later artist's Munich Self-Portrait is a riff on the Holy Face motif. This German artist of Praying Hands and The Four Apostles holds a thistle in an early self-portrait.

bonuses.leadin: This artist painted portraits of Edward Grimston and a young girl wearing a blue headdress with a strap of cloth under her chin. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Early Netherlandish artist who possibly depicted St. Eligius in his painting A Goldsmith in His Shop. He is often considered a pupil of Jan van Eyck, and dominated painting in Bruges after van Eyck’s death.

The fly Christus painted at the bottom of his Portrait of a Carthusian is an early example of this technique, in which an object is depicted so realistically that it appears to be three-dimensional. Its name is French for “fool the eye.”

The swooning position of Mary in Christus’s Lamentation echoes this earlier painter’s Deposition, which was commissioned by a guild of crossbowmen. His panels depicting the Justice of Herkenbald and the Justice of Trajan for the town hall of Brussels were much admired until they were destroyed in 1695.
